zh.Clun, Shropshire, England, United Kingdo: 英國英格蘭史洛普爾可倫
zh.lifeSummary: 當Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte於大約1046年出生在法國諾門迪奧內賽時，他的父親Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire26歲，母親Hugolina de Gernon21歲。他於1062年之前與Unnamed Wife de Saye在威爾士結婚。他們至少是3個兒子和1個女兒的父母。

zh-hans.Clun, Shropshire, England, United Kingdo: 英国英格兰史洛普爾可倫
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte于大约1046年出生在法国諾門迪奧內賽时，他的父亲Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire26岁，母亲Hugolina de Gernon21岁。他于1062年之前与Unnamed Wife de Saye在威尔士结婚。他们至少是3个儿子和1个女儿的父母。

nameMeanings.name: fitz
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (from Latin filius), used originally to distinguish a son from a father bearing the same personal name and then conventionalized as a hereditary surname, or short for a surname of which fiz ‘son’ was the first element, for example Fitzhenry and Fitzroy .
Altered form of English   Fitch  .
German: from a vernacular pet form of the personal name Vinzenz (see Vincent ) or Vitus (see Vito , compare Veit ). Compare also Fietz .

variants.name: Fitzgerald
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Eamon, Liam, Aidan, Bridgid, Caitlin, Donal, Fitz, Fitzgerald, Ciaran, Colum, Dermot.

Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Gerald (see Gerald ), ‘son of Gerald’. This is a very common Anglo-Norman name in Ireland, where it has been Gaelicized as Mac Gearailt. Irish bearers of the name claim descent from Gerald Fitzwalter de Windsor, constable of Pembroke Castle in the early 12th century, whose grandson Raymond (or Redmond) ‘le Gros’ FitzGerald (died after 1185) led the invasion of Ireland on behalf of Strongbow in 1170. A branch of the family held the title Knight of Glin from at least 1299–2011.

variants.name: Fitzwater
meanings: Irish: altered form of Fitzwalter, a patronymic from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Walter , meaning ‘son of Walter’.

History: This is the name of a family of Norman origin, descended from Godfrey, Count of Brionne (died c. 1010). Godfrey's grandson, Richard FitzGilbert, accompanied William the Conqueror and was made Lord of Clare. His great-grandson was Robert FitzWalter, one of the barons who guaranteed the Magna Carta in 1215.

variants.name: Fitzherbert
meanings: English and Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Herbert (see Herbert ), ‘son of Herbert’.

variants.name: Fitzhenry
meanings: Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Henry (see Henry ), ‘son of Henry’. This surname is common in Ireland (mainly Connacht), where it has been Gaelicized as Mac Éinrí. In at least some cases, it seems to have been adopted as a surname indicating the illegitimate offspring of a man called Henry.

variants.name: Fitzsimmons
meanings: Irish and English: variant of Fitzsimon, with post-medieval excrescent -s, from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + either of the personal names Simon or Sigmund (see Simon ), ‘son of Simon or Sigmund’. The name is also found in the Gaelicized form Mac Síomóin.

variants.name: Fitzgibbon
meanings: Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Gibbon, a Norman French pet form of Gilbert ; ‘son of Gibbon’. This name has long been a widespread and important surname in Ireland, and Gaelicized as Mac Giobúin. Sir Gilbert (Gibbon) FitzJohn, illegitimate son of John FitzGerald, 1st Baron Desmond (died 1261), was the ancestor of a line of Anglo-Norman aristocracy in Ireland, who acquired the surname Fitzgibbon with reference to him. Gilbert and his Fitzgibbon descendants were known as ‘the White Knights’ on account of their fair complexion.

variants.name: Fitzharris
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Aileen.

Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Harry ‘son of Harry’. MacLysaght describes this as the name of a distinguished County Wexford family, also known as Fitzhenry .

variants.name: Fitzmaurice
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Aidan, Ciaran, Colm.

Irish: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Maurice (see Morris 1), ‘son of Maurice’. This surname is common in Ireland, where it has also been Gaelicized as Mac Muiris. The Irish family of Fitzmaurice share a common Norman ancestry with the Fitzgerald family. They can be traced to Thomas Fitzmaurice, whose brother Gerald was the ancestor of the Irish Fitzgerald dynasty. Their father was Maurice Fitzgerald, a Norman baron (died 1176).

variants.name: Fitzhugh
meanings: English (Northamptonshire): from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (see Fitz ) + the personal name Hugh (see Hugh ), ‘son of Hugh’.

History: William Fitzhugh (1651–1701), from Bedford, England, emigrated to VA c. 1670 and established himself on the Potomac River in what was then Stafford County, VA, as a planter and exporter. He also practiced law, was a member of the Virginia House of Burgesses, and served in 1687 as lieutenant colonel of the county militia.

variants.name: Fitzroy
meanings: English: from Anglo-Norman French fi(t)z ‘son’ (from Latin filius), used originally to distinguish a son from a father bearing the same personal name and then conventionalized as a hereditary surname, or short for a surname of which fiz ‘son’ was the first element, for example Fitzhenry and Fitzroy .
Altered form of English   Fitch  .
German: from a vernacular pet form of the personal name Vinzenz (see Vincent ) or Vitus (see Vito , compare Veit ). Compare also Fietz .

nameMeanings.name: Picot
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Pierre, Francois, Jacques, Jeanpaul, Luc, Pascal, Yves.  Spanish Esperanza, Aida, Alicia, Ana, Felipe, Luis.
French: from Old French picot ‘pointed object, pickaxe’, a nickname for someone who used such an implement. This surname is also found in Haiti.
Catalan: nickname from Valencian dialect picot ‘(sheep or goat) bell’.

variants.name: Pickett
meanings: English:
from the Middle English, Old French personal name Picot, Pigot, a pet form of Pic (see Pike 6). In Middle English, the form Piket (Old French Picquet) was also common.
sometimes a late development of   Pickard   1 or 2.

variants.name: Picotte
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Honore, Andre, Jacques, Serge.
Altered form of French Picot , reflecting the Canadian and American French practice of sounding the final -t.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Say
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: of Norman origin, a habitational name from Sai in Orne or Say in Indre, France, perhaps so called from a Gaulish personal name Saius + the Latin locative suffix -acum (see also French name 3 below).

English: nickname, perhaps for someone who made or sold say, a type of woollen cloth, or for an habitual wearer of clothes made of this material, from Middle English sai(e) ‘say’ (Old French saie, from Latin saga, plural of sagum ‘military cloak’). See also Sayer .

French (southern): topographic name from saix (from Latin saxum) ‘rock’, or a habitational name from a place called with this word, for example, Say in Loire, Saix in Tarn and Vienne, Le Saix in Hautes-Alpes, or Les Saix in Isère.

variants.name: Sey
meanings: English and Scottish: probably a variant of   Say   particularly prevalent in Scotland.
Dutch and Flemish: from a short form of the ancient Germanic personal name Sigiheri (see Seger ).
West African (Ghana): Akan (Fante) name of unexplained etymology.

variants.name: Sayer
meanings: English: common occupational name from Middle English sayer, a phonetic variant of Sawyer , which in Wales and neighboring English counties may have been reinforced by the synonymous Welsh saer ‘carpenter’ and everywhere by a possible borrowing into Middle English of Old French seieor, saieur ‘sawyer’.

English: rare occupational name perhaps denoting an assayer of metals, or a servant who tasted his lord's food before serving, from Middle English saiour ‘assayer, tester’ (a shortened form of Old French assaiour, ultimately from Late Latin exagium, a derivative of exagmināre ‘to weigh’).
English: rare occupational name for a professional reciter or minstrel, from Middle English seier(e) ‘speaker’.

variants.name: Saye
meanings: English: variant of   Say  .
Americanized form of Slovenian Saje: nickname for someone with very dark, sooty hair, derived from the root of sajast ‘sooty’, or directly from saja ‘soot’.
West African (Liberia): from the personal name Saye, found among the Mano (Mah) people, denoting a first-born boy.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

en.lifeSummary: When Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte was born about 1046, in Sai, Orne, Normandy, France, his father, Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire, was 26 and his mother, Hugolina de Gernon, was 21. He married Unnamed Wife de Saye before 1062, in Wales. They were the parents of at least 3 sons and 1 daughter.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte est né environ 1046, à Sai, Orne, Normandie, France, son père, Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire, avait 26 ans et sa mère, Hugolina de Gernon, avait 21 ans. Il a épousé Unnamed Wife de Saye avant 1062, à Galles. Ils ont eu au moins 3 fils et 1 fille.

de.lifeSummary: Als Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte um 1046 in Sai, Orne, Normandie, Frankreich geboren wurde, war sein Vater Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire 26 und seine Mutter Hugolina de Gernon 21. Er hat vor 1062 in Wales Unnamed Wife de Saye geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 3 Söhne und 1 Tochter.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte è nato il circa 1046 nel Sai, Orne, Normandie, Francia, il suo padre, Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire, aveva 26 anni e il suo madre, Hugolina de Gernon, aveva 21 anni. Lui sposato Unnamed Wife de Saye prima del 1062, nel Galles. Erano genitori di almeno 3 figli e 1 figlia.

ja.lifeSummary: Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomteが約1046年にフランスノーマンディオルンサイで生まれた時，父Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshireは26歳，母Hugolina de Gernonは21歳であった。1062年以前にウェールズでUnnamed Wife de Sayeと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも3人の息子と1人の娘の親であった。

ko.lifeSummary: Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte(이)가 대략 1046년 프랑스 노먼디 른 사이에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire(은)는 26세였고, 그의 어머니 Hugolina de Gernon(은)는 21세였다. 그는 1062년 이전 웨일스에서 Unnamed Wife de Saye(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다.

mn.Clun, Shropshire, England, United Kingdo: Clun, Salopesberia, Англи, Нэгдсэн Вант Улс

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte nasceu aproximadamente 1046, em Sai, Orne, Normandie, França, seu pai, Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire, tinha 26 anos e sua mãe, Hugolina de Gernon, tinha 21 anos. Ele casou-se com Unnamed Wife de Saye antes 1062, em Gales. Eles tiveram pelo menos 3 filhos e 1 filha.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte родился около 1046 г. в местности сеи, орн, норманди, Франция, его отцу по имени Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire было 26 лет, а матери, которую звали Hugolina de Gernon, 21. Он женился на женщине по имени Unnamed Wife de Saye перед 1062 г. в местности Уэльс. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына и 1 дочь.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Robert fitz Picot de Say Lord of Clun, Vicomte nació aproximadamente 1046, en Sai, Orne, Normandie, Francia, su papá, Picot de Lascelles, I, High Sheriff of Cambridgeshire, tenía 26 años y su mamá, Hugolina de Gernon, tenía 21 años. Se casó con Unnamed Wife de Saye antes de 1062, en Gales. Fueron padres de por lo menos 3 hijos y 1 hija.
